skill.name: iac-checkov
skill.description: Run and interpret Checkov scans for Infrastructure as Code across Terraform, Kubernetes, CloudFormation, Dockerfile, Helm, and related formats. The skill provides scan commands, CI examples, compliance mapping, suppression guidance, custom policy patterns, and remediation-focused reporting workflows.
skill.summary: Use Checkov to find IaC misconfigurations, compliance gaps, and secrets before deployment.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, secret, and blocker keywords, but review found they are primarily Checkov usage examples, CI templates, and security policy terminology. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill still carries medium operational risk because it instructs users to run external scanners, read IaC files and reports, optionally download external modules, and use cloud security integrations.

medium_findings.title: External Scanner Commands Are Central to Skill Operation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users and CI systems to install and run Checkov, parse scan output with shell tools, and generate reports. This is legitimate for an IaC security skill, but it can execute local tools over large workspace paths and should be run only in trusted project contexts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is directly documented and operationally necessary. I found no evidence that command arguments are crafted for malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Optional Network Access and Cloud Integration
medium_findings.description: Examples enable downloading external Terraform modules and show optional Prisma Cloud or Bridgecrew integration through an API key variable. These patterns are legitimate, but they can contact third-party services and may expose metadata if configured carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit in configuration examples. It is bounded to documented Checkov integrations rather than hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: IaC and Secret Scan Outputs May Contain Sensitive Context
medium_findings.description: The skill encourages secrets scanning and report generation over infrastructure files. Scan inputs and generated JSON or SARIF reports can contain resource names, paths, misconfiguration details, and sometimes secret-like values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill plainly handles IaC files and scan reports. Sensitivity depends on the scanned repository and report retention settings.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Keywords Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: Several static hits for weak cryptography, command-and-control terminology, and Windows SAM are references to compliance controls, Checkov categories, or AWS SAM templates. They do not instruct the model to perform offensive activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show security documentation and cloud template terminology. No prompt injection or malicious operational instruction was present.

low_findings.title: Suppression Examples Require Governance
low_findings.description: The suppression guide includes examples that bypass specific Checkov checks for legacy or public resources. The guide also asks for justifications, but users could copy suppressions without approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are real and could reduce scanning coverage if misused. The surrounding guidance reduces but does not remove this risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell and CI Command Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many bash and CI snippets that execute Checkov, jq, package installation, and report generation commands. These are expected but should be reviewed before use in privileged CI environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command execution patterns are clearly present in executable examples. Their purpose matches the skill domain.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Enabled Dependency and Module Retrieval
dangerous_patterns.description: Configuration examples reference remote repositories, Checkov documentation, Prisma Cloud endpoints, and external Terraform module downloads. These can initiate network access when enabled by the user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints and module download option are explicit. I found no evidence of covert destinations or encoded exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Infrastructure teams need to catch cloud misconfigurations before they reach production. This skill guides Checkov scans, compliance mapping, CI gates, suppressions, and remediation reporting.
actual_capabilities: Provides Checkov commands for Terraform, Kubernetes, CloudFormation, Dockerfile, Helm, and serverless templates.
Explains how to generate CLI, JSON, SARIF, JUnit, and CycloneDX scan outputs.
Shows GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit examples for IaC security gates.
Maps common Checkov checks to CIS, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, NIST, and GDPR control areas.
Guides custom Checkov policy creation with Python and YAML examples.
Documents suppression practices with justifications, expiration dates, and compensating controls.
limitations: It depends on Checkov and related command-line tools being installed or available in CI.
It cannot prove cloud resources are safe after deployment without runtime or cloud account validation.
Compliance mappings are guidance and do not replace a formal audit.
Some examples need local path, framework, and severity tuning before production use.

use_cases.title: Pre-Merge IaC Security Gate
use_cases.description: Add Checkov scans to pull requests and fail builds when critical or high severity findings appear.

use_cases.title: Compliance Evidence Preparation
use_cases.description: Generate Checkov reports and map findings to common cloud security control families.

use_cases.title: Custom Policy Rollout
use_cases.description: Create organization-specific Checkov policies and load them through an external checks directory.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic IaC Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iac-checkov skill to scan my Terraform directory and explain the main security findings in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Pull Request Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iac-checkov skill to design a CI gate that fails on critical and high Checkov findings for Terraform and Kubernetes files.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Compliance Reporting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iac-checkov skill to generate a Checkov reporting workflow for CIS AWS and PCI-DSS evidence collection.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Custom Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iac-checkov skill to draft a custom Checkov policy that enforces required tags across AWS resources and explain how to test it.

output_examples.input: Scan a Terraform folder before merge.
output: A recommended Checkov command with Terraform framework selection and severity thresholds.
A short finding summary grouped by critical, high, medium, and skipped checks.
Remediation notes that identify the affected resources and safer configuration options.

output_examples.input: Create a CI workflow for IaC scanning.
output: A pipeline outline that installs Checkov, runs scans, saves artifacts, and fails on severe findings.
Guidance for publishing SARIF or JSON reports to the security review process.
Notes about report retention and avoiding unnecessary secret exposure.
A decision checklist covering business need, compensating controls, expiration, and approval owner.
A recommendation to narrow the suppression to one resource and one check where possible.

best_practices: Run scans on the smallest useful IaC scope and keep generated reports access controlled.
Fail CI on critical and high findings while tracking medium findings for remediation planning.
Require written justification, owner, and expiration for every Checkov suppression.
anti_patterns: Do not copy broad suppressions into production without security approval.
Do not enable external module downloads in CI without reviewing module source trust.
Do not publish raw Checkov reports if they include resource names, paths, or secret-like values.

faq.question: Does this skill run Checkov by itself?
faq.answer: It provides commands and workflow guidance. The user or automation environment must run Checkov with appropriate permissions.

faq.question: Which IaC formats does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Terraform, Kubernetes, CloudFormation, Dockerfile, Helm, ARM, serverless, and related Checkov-supported frameworks.

faq.question: Can it support compliance audits?
faq.answer: It can prepare Checkov evidence and control mappings, but a qualified auditor must validate final compliance conclusions.

faq.question: Can I use it in CI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit patterns for automated IaC scanning.

faq.question: How should suppressions be handled?
faq.answer: Use narrow suppressions with a specific reason, owner, approval path, and expiration date.

faq.question: What data should I protect?
faq.answer: Protect IaC files, scan reports, API keys, repository metadata, and any secret-like values detected during scans.
